The first disappearance was subtle—Mrs. Lillian Hart, a schoolteacher known for her kindness and her daily walks along the cliff path. Her absence was noticed only when the children failed to see her at the breakfast table. No trace of her was found, not even a footprint in the damp sand.

Elias Vorne arrived in Blackthorn Cove the next morning, his trench coat heavy with the weight of the sea mist. The town had a way of swallowing its own, he thought. He began his investigation at the school, speaking with the children and the other teachers, but the answers were sparse. No one had seen her leave. No one had heard her footsteps on the path that morning. It was as if she had vanished into the wind.

By midday, the second disappearance occurred. Mr. Dorian Granger, the lighthouse keeper, had not returned from his night shift. His boat was found adrift, the engine sputtering, the sails tattered. No one had seen him since the previous evening, when the fog had rolled in thick and fast.

The town had always been a place of quiet dread, its cobbled streets slick with salt and memory, but now it was a place of unease, where the air seemed to hold its breath.

It had started three months ago, with the disappearance of the fisherman, Jarek Morn. No boat, no body, just an empty cabin and a single, bloodstained net. Then came the young woman, Elise Rains, who vanished from the shore at midnight, her footprints leading into the surf, the tide swallowing them whole. And now, the latest case—Liam Duvall, the town’s only blacksmith, found in his forge, surrounded by tools and a single, unlit candle, as though he had frozen mid-thought.

**Title: *The Last Light of the Lighthouse***

The sea had always been a place of whispers.

For years, the town of Marisport had clung to the edge of the cliffs, its narrow streets and salt-stained buildings weathered by time and tide. The lighthouse, standing alone on the cliff’s edge, had been the town’s guardian for generations, its beam slicing through the dark like a blade. But now, it was silent.

Detective Elias Vane had never been a man of superstition. Still, when the mayor of Marisport came to him with a request, he felt the weight of something more than just a case.

“Three people have disappeared in the last six months,” the mayor said, his voice tight with unease. “All of them were last seen near the lighthouse. No trace, no footprints, no sign of struggle. Just… gone.”

Elias frowned, tapping his fingers against the wooden table. “And you think it’s connected to the lighthouse?”

The mayor hesitated. “We’ve heard the stories. The old ones say the lighthouse is cursed. That it takes people who come too close to its light. They vanish into the sea, never to return.”

Elias scoffed. “That’s just folklore. But I’ll look into it. I’ll need access to the lighthouse.”

The mayor looked relieved. “Of course. The lighthouse keeper has been absent for weeks.

**Title: *The Hollow Shore***
